completed_by: James Kim, Head of HR & Culture

top_training_priorities.description: Our productions consistently run over budget and behind schedule. Average overrun: 23% budget, 6 weeks delay. We have brilliant creative talent but lack discipline in planning, tracking, resource allocation. Problem: Creative people resist 'corporate' PM practices - see it as bureaucracy that kills creativity. Need: Project management adapted for creative industry - structure without stifling innovation. Target: 450 producers, production managers, department heads.
top_training_priorities.urgency: Critical - CEO mandate to improve within 6 months or heads roll

top_training_priorities.description: Entertainment industry has toxic legacy - power abuse, sexual harassment, bullying, impossible hours, exploitation. Board demanding culture change post-#MeToo. Current culture: hierarchical, ego-driven, 'creative genius' excuses bad behavior, long hours worn as badge of honor. Need: Transform to respectful, inclusive, sustainable culture while maintaining creative excellence. This is sensitive - people defensive, resistant, some deny problems exist. Need expert facilitators who understand entertainment industry dynamics.
top_training_priorities.urgency: High - regulatory pressure, legal liability, talent retention

top_training_priorities.description: Writers, directors, producers are artists who resist business thinking. Don't understand P&Ls, ROI, business strategy. Creates friction with finance team and poor business decisions. Example: Director spent $2M on single scene that ended up cut in edit - didn't understand budget implications. Need: Financial literacy and business fundamentals tailored for creative professionals. Frame as 'creative freedom requires business success' not 'abandon art for commerce'.

existing_programs: Safety training for set operations - well-received, practical
Software training (editing tools, VFX) - vendor-provided, technical
Leadership development - NONE except external conferences (sporadic)
current_training.what_works: Technical/software training works well - hands-on, immediate application. Safety training effective because clearly protects people.
current_training.what_doesn't: Anti-harassment training is ignored - seen as CYA legal requirement. No behavior change. Generic leadership programs from other industries don't resonate - entertainment is unique culture.

executives.needs: Strategic leadership, change management, board-level communication

senior_producers.needs: Advanced PM, team leadership, business strategy

mid_level_creatives.needs: Project management basics, collaboration, business fundamentals

success_metrics.project_performance: Reduce budget overruns from 23% average to <10%, Reduce schedule delays from 6 weeks avg to <2 weeks, Increase on-time/on-budget delivery rate from 32% to 75%
success_metrics.culture_indicators: Increase inclusion survey scores from 52% to 80%, Reduce HR complaints by 60%, Increase employee retention from 78% to 88%, Improve Glassdoor rating from 3.2 to 4.0+
success_metrics.business_results: Improve production efficiency (cost per hour of content) by 20%, Increase streaming content output by 40% without proportional cost increase, Maintain or improve creative quality metrics (awards, reviews, audience scores)

budget_timeline.timeline: Design: Feb-Mar 2026, Pilot: Apr-May 2026, Full rollout: Jun-Nov 2026
budget_timeline.constraints: Production schedules vary - people on set for months then off. Need flexible delivery. Peak production season is fall/winter. Contractors only available between projects.

special_considerations.entertainment_culture: Egos are real - famous directors, award-winning writers, celebrity actors. Training must respect their expertise and not feel condescending.
special_considerations.union_environment: Multiple unions (DGA, WGA, SAG-AFTRA, IATSE). Union rules impact scheduling, some training may need negotiation.
special_considerations.creative_resistance: Many creatives deeply skeptical of 'corporate' training - see it as antithetical to art. Must be positioned carefully.
special_considerations.project_based_work: People assembled for projects then disband. Knowledge doesn't transfer well between projects. Need to build organizational capability, not just individual.
special_considerations.24_7_production: Sets run around the clock. Office-hours training doesn't work for many employees. Need flexible access.
special_considerations.confidentiality: Projects are highly confidential (NDAs, competitive advantage). Training examples must respect this.

questions_for_consultant: Have you worked with entertainment/media companies before? Understand our unique culture?
How do you make PM training feel relevant to creatives vs corporate bureaucracy?
What's your approach to culture change in industries with toxic legacy?
Can you work with our complex union environment?
How do you train project-based workforce that's constantly assembling/disbanding?
What's your philosophy on work-life balance in industry known for long hours?
How do you measure behavior change vs just knowledge transfer?
Can you scale to train 1,400 contractors efficiently?
